definition: Define the skeleton of an algorithm in a base method, letting subclasses override specific steps without changing the overall flow.
ruleOfThumb: When multiple workflows share the same sequence but differ in a few steps, keep the sequence fixed and vary the hooks.

citation.author: Gamma, Helm, Johnson & Vlissides (GoF)
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: What repeated structure suggests this design could be improved?

options.text: Both classes repeat the same algorithm sequence with only one rendering step different, so the sequence should be centralized with customizable steps
options.explanation: Validation, transform, render, and audit order is duplicated. A template method captures that flow once and delegates variation points.

options.text: The issue is that render methods should return buffers instead of strings
options.explanation: Return format is a separate API choice, not the structural duplication highlighted.

options.text: Both classes should merge into one with if/else on output format for simplicity
options.explanation: A large conditional can reduce duplication short-term but often becomes harder to extend and maintain.

options.text: Audit should run before transform to catch raw input size
options.explanation: Order tweaks may be valid, but they do not address repeated workflow skeleton.

questions.prompt: The team has PDF, CSV, and XML export jobs. What pattern best avoids duplicating the shared pipeline?

options.text: Use a base job with a fixed run() sequence and subclass hooks for render step differences
options.explanation: All jobs share workflow order and vary primarily in output rendering. Template Method captures common sequence once.

options.text: Use copy-paste inheritance where each job keeps a full run() method for local clarity
options.explanation: Local clarity now becomes maintenance burden later when shared flow changes.

options.text: Use Strategy for every line of run() so no class needs a fixed sequence at all
options.explanation: Strategy can help with interchangeable algorithms, but this case benefits from a stable sequence with limited variation points.

options.text: Use one static helper function and pass the format name as a string
options.explanation: A string-based helper can still become a conditional hub and lose clear extension structure.

questions.prompt: Which design is more likely to break sequence consistency as more export types are added?

options.text: Version A — each class controls its own full sequence, so step order can drift and become inconsistent
options.explanation: B allows accidental reordering like render-before-clean in one subclass. A enforces one canonical algorithm structure.

options.text: Version B — abstract classes are rigid, so every export type should define complete workflows independently
options.explanation: Controlled rigidity is the benefit here: shared invariant sequence with explicit variation points.
